world_map.raw:
Center point: 37.9755° N, 23.7222° E (Athens)
Reach radius/trade zone description: Athenian silver coinage circulated across the entire Eastern Mediterranean and Near East — hoards found from Egypt to Turkey
Key nodes: Laurion mines (37.7167° N, 24.0500° E); Athenian Agora/Mint (37.9755° N, 23.7222° E); Piraeus port (37.9475° N, 23.6378° E); Delphi (38.4824° N, 22.5010° E); Elmalı, Turkey — site of 1984 dekadrachm hoard (36.7351° N, 29.9152° E)
Suggested map era: 600–400 BCE
Historical map source: Dipylon "Mapping Ancient Athens" GIS project; Barbié du Bocage 1785 map of Ancient Attica

quiz.q: What creature is depicted on the reverse of the Athenian tetradrachm?
Where did Athens get the silver for its most famous coins?
What did Athens build with the profits from the 483 BC Laurion silver strike?
About how many Athenian silver dekadrachms are known to survive today?
What ancient civilization first sourced Athens's earliest coin silver — from where?
quiz.a: Imported silver from places like Spain and Turkey, before Laurion was fully exploited

decision_simulation:
Scenario: It's 483 BC. Athens has just found a massive new silver vein at Laurion. Themistocles proposes spending the windfall on a war fleet instead of distributing it directly to citizens.
Options: (1) Distribute the silver directly to citizens as a cash dividend; (2) Build warships as Themistocles proposes; (3) Save the silver in the treasury
Historical outcome: Athens built the fleet — and those ships won the Battle of Salamis in 480 BC, saving Greece from Persian conquest and setting up Athens's naval empire
EXPLORE IT (CURRENTLY OPEN — highest priority)

understand.economic_impact:
One-line summary: Alexandros represents the very first time a government-backed coin became trusted enough to be used everywhere — the birth of money as we know it.
Three key economic shifts: (1) Standardized, government-guaranteed coinage replaced barter and ad-hoc weighed silver; (2) a natural resource windfall (the Laurion strike) was converted into state military and infrastructure investment rather than simply distributed; (3) reliable coinage enabled long-distance trade because merchants across the Mediterranean trusted the owl's silver content
Modern parallel: a national currency backed by a central bank's credibility — the way people trust a dollar or euro today because they trust the institution behind it
understand.political_impact:
One-line summary: The wealth from Laurion's silver mines funded the navy that saved Greek democracy from Persian conquest.
Three key political shifts: (1) Silver wealth let Athens fund a citizen-rowed navy instead of relying only on wealthy citizens for military power, which strengthened democracy; (2) coinage minted with the city's symbol (the owl) became a tool of civic identity and soft power abroad; (3) control over minting became a marker of sovereignty that later civilizations would imitate
Modern parallel: a country's currency as a symbol of national sovereignty and reserve-currency-style regional influence, the way the euro's design carries collective political identity today
understand.why_it_still_matters:
Plain-language answer: The coin in your pocket today exists because Athens figured out something 2,600 years ago — if people trust that a piece of metal is really worth what it says, they'll use it everywhere, and that trust can build empires. Money didn't start as government paper; it started as a promise stamped on silver.
Connection to today's world: Every time you trust that a dollar bill or a bank app number is "real" money, you're relying on the same kind of trust Alexandros's owl coin first earned from merchants across the ancient world.
